items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: Tooling cost is high but per-unit cost is very low at scale.
items.misconception: It is the cheapest method for any quantity.
items.random_wrong: It is the only way to make plastic items.
items.why: Students think a 'good' process is good for everything. Injection moulding has expensive moulds - it's only economical when costs are spread over thousands or millions of parts.

items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.correct: Designing products usable by the widest possible range of people without special adaptation.
items.random_wrong: Adding multiple language options to packaging.
items.why: Students conflate inclusive with specialist design. Inclusive design widens MAINSTREAM products to work for more people - the OXO Good Grips kitchen tools, designed for arthritic hands, work better for everyone.

items.q: For cutting metal you use a.
items.why: It's Hacksaw. “Coping saw” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: You must always wear what in the workshop?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Goggles. “Headphones” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many safety rules are listed in this lesson?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
